preamble: The shift from the pharyngeal 'ayin' (ʕ) to the glottal stop (ʔ) is a common weakening process known as de-pharyngealization. This shift simplifies the articulation by moving the point of constriction from the pharynx up to the glottis.

examples.note: Most modern speakers merge 'ayin' and 'aleph' (glottal stop) in Standard Israeli Hebrew.
Inscriptions show the merger of all gutturals into a simple glottal stop or zero.

sources: Sáenz-Badillos, A. (1993). A History of the Hebrew Language. https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/history-of-the-hebrew-language/D0DA01BEBF2C4A788904534B1D8240B7
Krahmalkov, C. R. (2001). A Phoenician-Punic Grammar. https://brill.com/display/title/6978?language=en
